Bearing 315

The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest
July/August 1998

Page 1

[Image: Graphic of a Pacific Northwest indigenous-style line art depicting a serpent or sea creature, enclosed in a box with the text: "1998 Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival" above it and "U.S. Classic Championships • U.S. Relay Championships. Cle Elum, Washington" below it.]

Registration deadline for competitive US Champs events at Cle Elum is July 25th. Recreational folks are welcome to register right up to the day of events August 7th, 8th and 9th. Of course, early registration by all is much appreciated by organizers!

Almost all volunteers are going to be able to run as well as help out. But, there are some positions that necessarily would conflict with competing. Like placing water on the courses or driving a shuttle for older and younger folks. So... if you have friends or family members that do not intend to compete, but would like to help out, please ask them to contact me: breseman@msn.com or (425) 483-6592. Thank you!

Rick Breseman
Meet Director

[Image: Decorative graphic of a triangle partially filled with gray, next to the section title.]

News and Notes

USOF 1997 Rankings
Bill Cusworth, USOF Rankings Coordinator

For those of you who have been waiting for the 1997 USOF Rankings, I am happy to report that I have made great progress in the last few weeks and have processed all US and Canadian A-meets (this is the first time that all Canadian A-meets have been included). I have calculated rank points for the Blue and Red courses and I have determined USOF member status and credit days for M21A and F21A only. I am currently working on the other courses and classes and estimate them to be finished July 6. In the interest of keeping this message short, I have included below just the top 10 in the M21A & F21A classes. The full list is available at: http://weber.u.washington.edu/~bill3/usof_rankings.html

Submissions for the next issue must be received by August 25, 1998. Send submissions to Don Denbo at dwd@halcyon.com or mail to 2138 N 186th St., Shoreline, WA, 98133.

1998 Nike World Masters Games
Glen Cafferty, Orienteering Commissioner

The Nike world masters are desperately trying to attract additional orienteers to the World Masters Games, 10-14 August, in Portland Oregon and Goldendale Washington. In addition to offering complementary registration to all Officials (we still need additional volunteers), they are offering a limited number of complementary registrations to orienteering groups of two or more orienteers.

Registration forms can be downloaded and printed from the website, "http://www.worldmasters.org".

Books on O
Harvey Friedman, Cascade Orienteering Club

With many new folks recently joining Cascade O. C., it is time to once again list books readily available at bookstores.

Page 2

  1. Be expert with Map and Compass by Born Kjellstrom 1976 Charles Scribner's Sons ISBN 0-684-14270-8 excellent for beginners, written at level for Boy Scouts
  2. The Penguin Book of Orienteering by Roger Smith 1982 Penguin Books --- for adult beginners, contains British O history
  3. Orienteering for Sport and Pleasure by Bengtsson and Atkinson 1977 The Stephen Greene Press ISBN 0-8289-0270-4, available at many branches of Seattle Library, more detail on how to improve your skills than 1) & 2)
  4. Orienteering by John Disley 1978 Stackpole Books ISBN 0-8117-2023-3 --- similar level as 3), similar British style as 2)
  5. Cross-country Navigation by Phillips, Phillips, & Foley 1989 --- written by Australian rogainers, it may still be available locally

There are more specialized books which folks who go to Class A meets have brought back with them or they are available by mail from the O vendors such as A&E Enterprises or Orienteering Unlimited.

Sacajawea does Training!
Frank Skorina, Sacajawea Orienteers

Sacajawea Orienteers' most used map is our newest map. First printed in April 1998, it is in Columbia County, Washington (pop. 4000) and travel to a big town is a 75 mile trip to Walla Walla or slightly longer to Pullman. The map covers a modest 0.15 square km at a scale of 1:2000 and is printed in a single color.

The map is of the Camp Wooten Environmental Learning Center on the Tucannon River in the Umatilla National Forest. School districts often rent the camp for the better part of a week, frequently for their 5th or 6th graders. I help out those schools by introducing the kids to map reading and the hunting for orange and white flags.

My instruction is fairly basic. I wipe their puzzled looks off their faces by explaining what orienteering is. I entertain them with the "Orienteering, All Welcome" video. I show them equipment and maps and orienting. And by that time I hope they are squirming in their seats to do an eight-control score-O.

This year I have had three groups for a total of 388 participants, and have one group scheduled for the fall. I try to balance helping the schools and introducing more of the masses to orienteering with taking a day off of work. So far it is working and it is great fun to be with the kids.

Date School District #
April 23, 1998 Dayton, Touchet, Starbuck 102
May 7, 1998 North Franklin County 165
May 27, 1998 Prosser 121

Help Wanted

Interested in a career in mapmaking? Walker Associates has a position available in their graphics editing department. Looking for someone with experience in AutoCAD, OCAD, or MicroStation. Send resumes to:

Walker Associates, Attn: David Tomita

[Image: Decorative graphic of a triangle partially filled with gray, next to the section title.]

Upcoming Events

University of Washington: Canoe and Foot Orienteering

On Sunday July 12, we will have a combined foot/canoe orienteering event using both the Union Bay and University of Washington Maps. You can do either the land course, or the canoe course or make a duathalon and do both. Land courses will be beginner (0.7 Km), and short and medium intermediate (3 and 4.2 Km) Canoe rentals are available at the University Waterfront Activities Center (WAC), for $5.00 per hour for non students. You can rent a canoe anytime after 9 am on Sundays. The WAC is located on the eastern side of the University parking lots E11 and E12 which are on the south side of Husky stadium. There is normally no charge for UW parking on Sundays. Starts will be just southwest of the WAC, near the climbing rock, from 10 am till noon. Meet Director and Canoe Course: Rick Hood. Land Courses: Don Atkinson

[Image: Decorative graphic of a triangle partially filled with gray, next to the section title.]

Presidents Column

by Rick Hood, COC President

While there are many great O options this summer, COC is really gearing up for the United States Orienteering Federation Championships, hosted by us August 7-9 on our new maps in the Cle Elum area. This event will be a lot of fun, whether your aim is to shoot for a championship medal, to meet orienteers from across the US, or to just enjoy a great course on a good map. We really hope you are planning to be a part of this event.

So, sign up now. If you need a form or have some questions, please call Bill Cusworth at (206) 789-1346. Better yet, if you would like to help, please call Rick Breseman at (425) 483-6592.

Congratulations to Eric Bone. He must have a thing for "vice". Not only is he the VP of COC, but he has just recently been elected the VP for Competition for our national organization, USOF. (It is also rumored that he watches "Vice-Squad" on TV.)

Speaking of congratulations, USOF gave a big "thumbs up" to Mike and Vivien Fritz. The national organization president, Gary Kraght, awarded Mike and Vivien a "President's Award" for outstanding volunteerism at the Veteran's World Cup last fall in Minnesota. (You can pass along your own congratulations: Mike and Vivien will be working the finish at the US Champs next month.)

Changing directions, you might have noticed our hotline machine has been acting up a bit; sorry for this. As soon as we find a machine that will meet our needs, we will replace it. (You might be interested to know that the state orienteering organization, WSOA, was kind enough to award us a grant to do such.) Meanwhile, when you call (206) 783-3866, the main mailbox will have the information about our next few meets.

Oh, there is some truth to the rumor that Bearing 315 Editor, Don Denbo, will be including some photos of the US Champs in the next issue. However, the BEST way to experience the champs is first hand; see you there.

Western Washington University

April 26, 1998
Bellingham, WA
Chuckanut Orienteering Club

Meet Director: Ken Klepsch
Course Setter: Roger Mertens
Registration: Johanne Klepsch
Timing: Johanne Klepsch

This meet didn't make the March/April 315 in time. However, the local publicity was sufficient that I thought we would get a better turnout. We also plan on contouring and updating this map, really! Thank you Halters. Roger Mertens took his first shot at designing and setting courses; he performed well under pressure. For those of you that don't know Roger, one of his many volunteer activities is working with Greyhound Pets, Inc. Finding homes for these gentle, retired racers is a real labor of love.

Moran State Park

May 2, 1998
Orcas Is., WA
Cascade Orienteering Club

It was a beautiful sunny yet cool day up top Mt. Constitution. Robert and I placed 27 controls and participants had 90 minutes to find as many as they could in any order they wished. Several runners nearly got all controls. Penalty for arriving back late was one control per minute overtime. As course designers our goal was to set up a choice of legs and make it challenging by using one of a set of parallel features.

There is no easy way to set beginner courses, in this area, so we held a training clinic for those just starting out in orienteering. I want to commend all the juniors who participated in this meet. Moran State Park is a very challenging area due to the fact that the map has a lot of detail. All of the Juniors did very well with this tricky navigation. Thanks to all those listed below who volunteered their efforts.

With great weather and a contingent coming back over from Moran State Park, this was the most enjoyable event I've worked with CHUCKO. Bill Green took the heat off of me by designing and setting the courses, printing legible description sheets, and eliciting feedback from the participants. Everyone waited to see if Bill Cusworth could overtake Paul Hoopman for top finisher on Course 4. Despite finding the local brewpub the night before, Bill surged ahead by 3:25. We wouldn't mind combining this event with Moran St. Park again.

Rooks Park

May 10, 1998
Walla Walla, WA
Sacajawea Orienteers

Meet Director & Course Setter: Frank Skorina

The facilities at Rooks Park did not actually open for a few days, but along the road leading into the park, there are plenty of parking places and a good place to set up meet central from the back of a van. This means that the starts were slightly off the north end of the map. For the advanced course, I placed control No. 9 just a few meters from the southern end of the map. This psychological ploy of requiring participant to completely traverse a whole map did not slow Sergey Velichko, as he showed a superior physiological and cerebral capacity, blazing through the advanced course in 42:42 for an average rate of 5:42 per km.

To put this into perspective, imagine running along on flat pavement at a nice 5:00 per km (8:00 per mile) clip. Now take away the pavement and replace it with knee high grass between a few trails in rolling hills. Your way is not marked (no crossing guards here) so you need to carry a map with the 14 check points listed. Now add 15% to 30% to the distance because the best route may not be the bee-line. Would that increase your average rate by more than 42 seconds per km? It certainly would mine.

Velichko, coming from Boise, wanted to make the most of his trip so was not content with just the advanced course. He also did the intermediate and made a run on the beginners course, finishing with an unofficial time of just over 8 minutes.

Also on the long course was first time orienteer Adam Fyall, a Benton County planner and tri-athlete, training for the Canadian and Swiss Ironmans. He gave up on two controls citing a lack of patience, something perhaps lacking in triathlon training. Club member Chris Howard also missed a control, needing to arrive home not too much later than his promised arrival time.

On the intermediate course, WW High School Freshman Mark VanDonge needed to make up some of Mrs. See's PE classes due to an emergency (continued on page 4)

Page 4

(Rooks Park continued)
appendectomy earlier in the year. Dan Varheis came up from Pendleton and used the course to loosen up his hips and joined local Dennis Mahaffery. It was the first meet for the three and we hope to see them again soon.

We also welcome the Hamilton family as new members.

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for loan (with deposit) at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

  • [ ] $25 extra airmail

What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!

Bearing 315 is
Published by:

Cascade Orienteering Club
Post Office Box 31375
Seattle, Washington 98103
(206) 783-3866

block_index.text: The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest July/August 1998

[Image: Graphic of a Pacific Northwest indigenous-style line art depicting a serpent or sea creature, enclosed in a box with the text: "1998 Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival" above it and "U.S. Classic Championships • U.S. Relay Championships. Cle Elum, Washington" below it.]

Registration deadline for competitive US Champs events at Cle Elum is July 25th. Recreational folks are welcome to register right up to the day of events August 7th, 8th and 9th. Of course, early registration by all is much appreciated by organizers!

Almost all volunteers are going to be able to run as well as help out. But, there are some positions that necessarily would conflict with competing. Like placing water on the courses or driving a shuttle for older and younger folks. So... if you have friends or family members that do not intend to compete, but would like to help out, please ask them to contact me: breseman@msn.com or (425) 483-6592. Thank you!
[Image: Decorative graphic of a triangle partially filled with gray, next to the section title.]
USOF 1997 Rankings Bill Cusworth, USOF Rankings Coordinator

For those of you who have been waiting for the 1997 USOF Rankings, I am happy to report that I have made great progress in the last few weeks and have processed all US and Canadian A-meets (this is the first time that all Canadian A-meets have been included). I have calculated rank points for the Blue and Red courses and I have determined USOF member status and credit days for M21A and F21A only. I am currently working on the other courses and classes and estimate them to be finished July 6. In the interest of keeping this message short, I have included below just the top 10 in the M21A & F21A classes. The full list is available at: http://weber.u.washington.edu/~bill3/usof_rankings.html

Copyright 1998, Cascade Orienteering Club. Permission is given to reproduce for the promotion of orienteering. Bearing 315 is published six times each year by Cascade Orienteering Club. Submissions for the next issue must be received by August 25, 1998. Send submissions to Don Denbo at dwd@halcyon.com or mail to 2138 N 186th St., Shoreline, WA, 98133.
1998 Nike World Masters Games Glen Cafferty, Orienteering Commissioner

The Nike world masters are desperately trying to attract additional orienteers to the World Masters Games, 10-14 August, in Portland Oregon and Goldendale Washington. In addition to offering complementary registration to all Officials (we still need additional volunteers), they are offering a limited number of complementary registrations to orienteering groups of two or more orienteers.
Registration forms can be downloaded and printed from the website, "http://www.worldmasters.org".
Books on O Harvey Friedman, Cascade Orienteering Club
With many new folks recently joining Cascade O. C., it is time to once again list books readily available at bookstores.
Be expert with Map and Compass by Born Kjellstrom 1976 Charles Scribner's Sons ISBN 0-684-14270-8 excellent for beginners, written at level for Boy Scouts
The Penguin Book of Orienteering by Roger Smith 1982 Penguin Books --- for adult beginners, contains British O history

Orienteering for Sport and Pleasure by Bengtsson and Atkinson 1977 The Stephen Greene Press ISBN 0-8289-0270-4, available at many branches of Seattle Library, more detail on how to improve your skills than 1) & 2)
Orienteering by John Disley 1978 Stackpole Books ISBN 0-8117-2023-3 --- similar level as 3), similar British style as 2)
Cross-country Navigation by Phillips, Phillips, & Foley 1989 --- written by Australian rogainers, it may still be available locally
There are more specialized books which folks who go to Class A meets have brought back with them or they are available by mail from the O vendors such as A&E Enterprises or Orienteering Unlimited.
Sacajawea does Training! Frank Skorina, Sacajawea Orienteers

Sacajawea Orienteers' most used map is our newest map. First printed in April 1998, it is in Columbia County, Washington (pop. 4000) and travel to a big town is a 75 mile trip to Walla Walla or slightly longer to Pullman. The map covers a modest 0.15 square km at a scale of 1:2000 and is printed in a single color.

The map is of the Camp Wooten Environmental Learning Center on the Tucannon River in the Umatilla National Forest. School districts often rent the camp for the better part of a week, frequently for their 5th or 6th graders. I help out those schools by introducing the kids to map reading and the hunting for orange and white flags.

My instruction is fairly basic. I wipe their puzzled looks off their faces by explaining what orienteering is. I entertain them with the "Orienteering, All Welcome" video. I show them equipment and maps and orienting. And by that time I hope they are squirming in their seats to do an eight-control score-O.

This year I have had three groups for a total of 388 participants, and have one group scheduled for the fall. I try to balance helping the schools and introducing more of the masses to orienteering with taking a day off of work. So far it is working and it is great fun to be with the kids.

Interested in a career in mapmaking? Walker Associates has a position available in their graphics editing department. Looking for someone with experience in AutoCAD, OCAD, or MicroStation. Send resumes to:
Walker Associates, Attn: David Tomita 12652 Interurban Av. S. Seattle, WA. 98168 fax: 206-244-2333 or e-mail: walkermap@walkermap.com
[Image: Decorative graphic of a triangle partially filled with gray, next to the section title.]
University of Washington: Canoe and Foot Orienteering

On Sunday July 12, we will have a combined foot/canoe orienteering event using both the Union Bay and University of Washington Maps. You can do either the land course, or the canoe course or make a duathalon and do both. Land courses will be beginner (0.7 Km), and short and medium intermediate (3 and 4.2 Km) Canoe rentals are available at the University Waterfront Activities Center (WAC), for $5.00 per hour for non students. You can rent a canoe anytime after 9 am on Sundays. The WAC is located on the eastern side of the University parking lots E11 and E12 which are on the south side of Husky stadium. There is normally no charge for UW parking on Sundays. Starts will be just southwest of the WAC, near the climbing rock, from 10 am till noon. Meet Director and Canoe Course: Rick Hood. Land Courses: Don Atkinson
[Image: Decorative graphic of a triangle partially filled with gray, next to the section title.]

While there are many great O options this summer, COC is really gearing up for the United States Orienteering Federation Championships, hosted by us August 7-9 on our new maps in the Cle Elum area. This event will be a lot of fun, whether your aim is to shoot for a championship medal, to meet orienteers from across the US, or to just enjoy a great course on a good map. We really hope you are planning to be a part of this event.
So, sign up now. If you need a form or have some questions, please call Bill Cusworth at (206) 789-1346. Better yet, if you would like to help, please call Rick Breseman at (425) 483-6592.

Congratulations to Eric Bone. He must have a thing for "vice". Not only is he the VP of COC, but he has just recently been elected the VP for Competition for our national organization, USOF. (It is also rumored that he watches "Vice-Squad" on TV.)

Speaking of congratulations, USOF gave a big "thumbs up" to Mike and Vivien Fritz. The national organization president, Gary Kraght, awarded Mike and Vivien a "President's Award" for outstanding volunteerism at the Veteran's World Cup last fall in Minnesota. (You can pass along your own congratulations: Mike and Vivien will be working the finish at the US Champs next month.)

Changing directions, you might have noticed our hotline machine has been acting up a bit; sorry for this. As soon as we find a machine that will meet our needs, we will replace it. (You might be interested to know that the state orienteering organization, WSOA, was kind enough to award us a grant to do such.) Meanwhile, when you call (206) 783-3866, the main mailbox will have the information about our next few meets.

Oh, there is some truth to the rumor that Bearing 315 Editor, Don Denbo, will be including some photos of the US Champs in the next issue. However, the BEST way to experience the champs is first hand; see you there.
[Image: Decorative graphic of a triangle partially filled with gray, next to the section title.]
April 26, 1998 Bellingham, WA Chuckanut Orienteering Club
Meet Director: Ken Klepsch Course Setter: Roger Mertens Registration: Johanne Klepsch Timing: Johanne Klepsch Permission: Tjalling Ypma

This meet didn't make the March/April 315 in time. However, the local publicity was sufficient that I thought we would get a better turnout. We also plan on contouring and updating this map, really! Thank you Halters. Roger Mertens took his first shot at designing and setting courses; he performed well under pressure. For those of you that don't know Roger, one of his many volunteer activities is working with Greyhound Pets, Inc. Finding homes for these gentle, retired racers is a real labor of love.
May 2, 1998 Orcas Is., WA Cascade Orienteering Club

It was a beautiful sunny yet cool day up top Mt. Constitution. Robert and I placed 27 controls and participants had 90 minutes to find as many as they could in any order they wished. Several runners nearly got all controls. Penalty for arriving back late was one control per minute overtime. As course designers our goal was to set up a choice of legs and make it challenging by using one of a set of parallel features.

There is no easy way to set beginner courses, in this area, so we held a training clinic for those just starting out in orienteering. I want to commend all the juniors who participated in this meet. Moran State Park is a very challenging area due to the fact that the map has a lot of detail. All of the Juniors did very well with this tricky navigation. Thanks to all those listed below who volunteered their efforts.

Meet Director: Sandy Kish Miller Course Design: Robert Miller/Sandy Kish Miller Registration: Bill Cusworth General Help: Marjorie Stratton Marker Pickup: Don Atkinson, Lawrence Griffith, Bill Cusworth, Paul Hoopman, Tracy Doherty, Tony Lathan, Jim Doggett, Sarah Lillis, Travis Egbert, Anna Orr, John Faubion, Ginger Stratton, Peter Golde, Dan Waugh
May 3, 1998 Anacortes, WA Chuckanut Orienteering Club
Meet Director: Ken Klepsch Course Setter: Bill Green Registration & BBQ: Johanne Klepsch Timing: Bill Green & Roger Mertens Control Pickup: Bill Cusworth, Tracy Doherty, Sarah Lillis

With great weather and a contingent coming back over from Moran State Park, this was the most enjoyable event I've worked with CHUCKO. Bill Green took the heat off of me by designing and setting the courses, printing legible description sheets, and eliciting feedback from the participants. Everyone waited to see if Bill Cusworth could overtake Paul Hoopman for top finisher on Course 4. Despite finding the local brewpub the night before, Bill surged ahead by 3:25. We wouldn't mind combining this event with Moran St. Park again.
May 10, 1998 Walla Walla, WA Sacajawea Orienteers
Meet Director & Course Setter: Frank Skorina

The facilities at Rooks Park did not actually open for a few days, but along the road leading into the park, there are plenty of parking places and a good place to set up meet central from the back of a van. This means that the starts were slightly off the north end of the map. For the advanced course, I placed control No. 9 just a few meters from the southern end of the map. This psychological ploy of requiring participant to completely traverse a whole map did not slow Sergey Velichko, as he showed a superior physiological and cerebral capacity, blazing through the advanced course in 42:42 for an average rate of 5:42 per km.

To put this into perspective, imagine running along on flat pavement at a nice 5:00 per km (8:00 per mile) clip. Now take away the pavement and replace it with knee high grass between a few trails in rolling hills. Your way is not marked (no crossing guards here) so you need to carry a map with the 14 check points listed. Now add 15% to 30% to the distance because the best route may not be the bee-line. Would that increase your average rate by more than 42 seconds per km? It certainly would mine.

Velichko, coming from Boise, wanted to make the most of his trip so was not content with just the advanced course. He also did the intermediate and made a run on the beginners course, finishing with an unofficial time of just over 8 minutes.

Also on the long course was first time orienteer Adam Fyall, a Benton County planner and tri-athlete, training for the Canadian and Swiss Ironmans. He gave up on two controls citing a lack of patience, something perhaps lacking in triathlon training. Club member Chris Howard also missed a control, needing to arrive home not too much later than his promised arrival time.
On the intermediate course, WW High School Freshman Mark VanDonge needed to make up some of Mrs. See's PE classes due to an emergency (continued on page 4)

(Rooks Park continued) appendectomy earlier in the year. Dan Varheis came up from Pendleton and used the course to loosen up his hips and joined local Dennis Mahaffery. It was the first meet for the three and we hope to see them again soon.
We also welcome the Hamilton family as new members.
Darci, Dan, Zak Hamilton, & Donna Kennedy
Erik, Matt, Russell Skorina, & Holly Howard
Meet Director & Course Setter: Ivar Husa

Ivar served us up a grand meet on a new map with long, real long courses. There were lots of minutes on the advanced and intermediate courses with everyone enjoying at least two hours of navigation under sunny, breezy skies.
May 23, 1998 Cle Elum, WA Sammamish Orienteering Club

@ Inland Northwest Cup winners # Washington State Champ
Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for loan (with deposit) at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair of socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Event fees are charged on a per-registration basis: two people running together with the same map and punch card pay a single fee. Additional maps are available; the charge varies depending on the map. Fees vary, but the following are typical fees for local ("C") events with color maps. "A" and "B" meets, state championships, rogaines, and other events will cost somewhat more.
Course Setting | Field Checking | Exploration of new areas

As a U.S. Citizen or U.S. Resident, I hereby apply for USOF membership. I understand that I am entitled to a one-year subscription to Orienteering North America, voting rights, and membership in the IOF. Please print.
Member of a chartered club? Club __________________
[ ] Individual $25 member of a chartered club
[ ] Family $30 members of a chartered club
[ ] Junior $5 (18 & under / no vote / no magazine)

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!
